name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١)
```

```english
O Prophet! Be wary of Allah and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (1)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (٢)
```

```english
Follow that which is revealed to you from your Lord. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do. (2)
```

```arabic
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (٣)
```

```english
And put your trust in Allah; Allah suffices as trustee. (3)
```

```arabic
مَّا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِّن قَلْبَيْنِ فِى جَوْفِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْوَٰجَكُمُ ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى تُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَآءَكُمْ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِكُمْ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَقُولُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِى ٱلسَّبِيلَ (٤)
```

```english
Allah has not put two hearts within any man, nor has He made your wives whom you repudiate by zihar your mothers, nor has he made your adopted sons your [actual] sons. These are mere utterances of your mouths. But Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way. (4)
```

```arabic
ٱدْعُوهُمْ لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَمَوَٰلِيكُمْ ۚ وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَآ أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٥)
```

```english
Call them after their fathers. That is more just with Allah. If you do not know their fathers, then they are your brethren in the faith and your kinsmen. Excepting what your hearts may intend deliberately, there will be no sin upon you for any mistake that you may make therein. And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
```

```arabic
ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُۥٓ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ ۗ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ إِلَّآ أَن تَفْعَلُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا (٦)
```

```english
The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers. The blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and Emigrants, barring any favour you may do your comrades. This has been written in the Book. (6)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (٧)
```

```english
[Recall] when We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary, and We took from them a solemn pledge, (7)
```

```arabic
لِّيَسْـَٔلَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (٨)
```

```english
so that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness. And He has prepared for the faithless a painful punishment. (8)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah contains various fragments of knowledge (المعارف), rulings (الأحكام), stories (القصص), lessons (العبر), and exhortations (المواعظ). It includes the account of the Battle of the Trench (غزوة الخندق) and an allusion to the story of the Banu Qurayzah from among the Jews. The context of its verses testifies that it was among those revealed in Medina.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا» **O Prophet! Be God-conscious, and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise.** (33:1). This is a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to maintain godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure* — and it serves as a preamble to the prohibition that follows: «وَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ» **and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites.**

The context of the prohibition—combining the disbelievers (الكافرين) and the hypocrites (المنافقين) and forbidding obedience to them—reveals that the disbelievers were asking him for something that Allah, the Glorified, did not approve of, while the hypocrites were supporting them in their request and being persistent. It was a matter concerning which Allah, the Glorified, by His knowledge and wisdom, had already decreed otherwise, and divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) had descended to the contrary. It was a grave matter where the alignment of external factors might have favored the opposite outcome unless Allah willed otherwise. Thus, He warned the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) against granting their request and commanded him to follow what Allah revealed to him and to rely upon Him.

This supports what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول): that a number of the chieftains of Quraysh, after the Battle of Uhud, entered Medina under a guarantee of safety from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and asked him to leave them and their idols alone, in exchange for which they would leave him and his God alone. Consequently, these verses were revealed, and the Prophet did not grant them that request, as will be discussed in the following traditional report.

From what has preceded, the reason for concluding the verse with His saying: «إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا» **Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise** becomes clear, as does the reason for following this verse with the two verses after it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «وَاتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا» **Follow what is revealed to you from your Lord; indeed Allah is well-aware of what you do.** (33:2).

The verse is general in its own right, but because it occurs within the context of the prohibition, it commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to follow what has been sent down through revelation (الوحي) regarding what the disbelievers and hypocrites were asking of him. His "following" it means implementing it in action, as evidenced by His saying: «إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا» **indeed Allah is well-aware of what you do.**

**His saying, the Exalted**: «وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا» **And put your trust in Allah; Allah suffices as a trustee.** (33:3). This verse is like the preceding one in that it is general in its own right. However, because it occurs in the context of the previous prohibition, it indicates a command to place trust (التوكل) — *the act of relying solely upon Allah while delegating one's affairs to Him* — in Allah regarding what the revelation commands him. It suggests that the matter is difficult to achieve when considering apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية), such that the heart cannot remain safe from the onset of fear and agitation except through trust in Allah, the Glorified; for He is the only cause (السبب) that no opposing cause can overcome.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «مَّا جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِّن قَلْبَيْنِ فِي جَوْفِهِ» **Allah has not put two hearts within any man.** (33:4). This is an allusion (كناية) to the impossibility of combining two mutually exclusive beliefs (المتنافيين في الاعتقاد). A single heart—meaning a single soul (النفس الواحدة)—cannot accommodate two incompatible beliefs or two contradictory opinions. If there were two incompatible things, they would belong to two hearts, but Allah has not placed two hearts within any man's breast. Thus, a single man cannot believe in two incompatible things or affirm two contradictions. His saying: «فِي جَوْفِهِ» **within his breast** (literally: in his hollow) serves to emphasize the point, similar to His saying: «وَلَٰكِن تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ» **but it is the hearts in the breasts that turn blind.** (22:46).

It has been said that the sentence is a preamble and preparation, acting as a justification (التعليل) for what follows regarding the annulment of the practice of *zihar* (الظهار) and adoption (التبني). In *zihar*, a man would treat his wife as being in the position of his mother, and in adoption and calling someone a son, he would treat another's child as his own. Combining the status of wife and mother, as well as combining the sonship of another with one's own sonship, is a combination of incompatible things. They cannot coexist except in two hearts, and Allah has not placed two hearts within any man.

It is not improbable that the sentence serves as a justification for His previous saying: «لَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ» **do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites** and «وَاتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ» **Follow what is revealed to you from your Lord.** This is because obedience to Allah and His guardianship (ولايته), and obedience to the disbelievers and hypocrites and their guardianship, are incompatible and distinct, like monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and polytheism (الشرك). They cannot coexist in a single heart, and Allah has not placed two hearts within any man.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْواجَكُمُ اللَّائِي تُظاهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ» **nor has He made your wives whom you repudiate by zihar your mothers.** (33:4). In the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), a man would say to his wife, "You are to me like the back of my mother," or "Your back is to me like the back of my mother." He would liken her back to his mother's back, and this was called *zihar* (ظهارا) and was considered a divorce (طلاقا) for her. Islam annulled this.

The meaning of the verse is that Allah did not make your wives, from whom you separate by saying "Your back is to me like the back of my mother," into mothers for you. Since He did not make them so, there is no effect to this statement; the "making" (الجعل) here is legislative (تشريعي).

**His saying, the Exalted**: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ» **nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons.** (33:4). **The adopted sons** (الأدعياء) is the plural of *da'iyy* (دعي), which refers to one taken as a son and called a son. Calling others sons and adoption (التبني) were prevalent among them in the Age of Ignorance, as well as among the advanced nations of that time like Rome and Persia. They used to apply the rulings of biological sons (الولد الصلبي) to the adopted son, such as inheritance (التوارث), the prohibition of marriage, and other matters. Islam annulled this.

The meaning of the verse is that Allah did not make those whom you claim for yourselves as sons into your actual sons, such that the rulings applicable to biological sons would apply to them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَاهِكُمْ ۖ وَاللَّهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ» **These are mere words of your mouths; but Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way.** (33:4). The demonstration (الإشارة) in His saying: «ذَٰلِكُمْ» **These** refers to what preceded regarding *zihar* and adoption, or to adoption alone, which is more apparent and is supported by the fact that the following verse is restricted to the ruling on adoption only.

His saying: «قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَاهِكُمْ» **mere words of your mouths** means that the attribution of the adopted son to yourselves is nothing but a statement you utter with your mouths, having no reality beyond that. It is an allusion to the absence of any actual effect, as in His saying: «كَلَّا ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَائِلُهَا» **No! It is just a word he utters.** (23:100).

His saying: «وَاللَّهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ» **but Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way.** The meaning of His speech being "the truth" (الحق) is that if He informs of something, reality corresponds to what He has informed; and if He initiates a ruling, its effects follow, and it corresponds to the actual benefit (المصلحة الواقعية).

The meaning of His guiding to the way (هدايته السبيل) is that He directs the one He guides to the path of truth (سبيل الحق) which contains goodness and felicity. In these two sentences, there is an intimation to leave your own words and take His word.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «ادْعُوهُمْ لِآبَائِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ اللَّهِ» **Call them by [the names of] their fathers; that is more just with Allah.** (33:5) to the end of the verse. The preposition *lam* in «لِآبَائِهِمْ» denotes exclusivity (الاختصاص); meaning, call them while they are exclusively attributed to their fathers. His saying: «هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ اللَّهِ» **that is more just with Allah**, the pronoun refers to the verbal noun (المصدر) understood from His saying: «ادْعُوهُمْ» **Call them**, similar to His saying: «اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ» **Be just; that is nearer to godwariness.** (5:8). **More just** (أقسط) is a superlative form of *qist* (القسط), meaning justice (العدل).

The meaning is: Attribute them to their fathers—when you call them—because calling them by their fathers is more just in the sight of Allah.

His saying: «فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوا آبَاءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَمَوَالِيكُمْ» **But if you do not know their fathers, then they are your brethren in faith and your wards.** (33:5). What is meant by not knowing their fathers is not knowing their specific identities. **The wards** (الموالي) are the protégés or allies (الأولياء). The meaning is: If you do not know their fathers, do not attribute them to anyone other than their fathers; rather, call them by brotherhood and religious guardianship (الولاية الدينية).

His saying: «وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِ وَلَٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ» **There is no sin upon you in what you may mistake therein, but [only in] what your hearts intend.** (33:5). That is, there is no sin upon you for what you did by mistake due to oversight or forgetfulness, such as calling them by other than their fathers; but that which your hearts intended is a sin—or rather, the intentionality of your hearts in that matter contains the sin.

His saying: «وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا» **And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.** (33:5) refers to what was done by mistake.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ» **The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers.** (33:6). The "souls of the believers" are the believers themselves. Thus, the meaning of the Prophet being "closer" (أولى) to them than their own souls is that he has more right over them than they have over themselves. The meaning of this "priority" (الأولوية) is the preponderance of one side when a choice must be made between it and that which it has priority over. The result is that whatever a believer deems for himself in terms of protection, care, love, honor, responding to a call, and executing a will, the Prophet has more right to that than the believer's own self. If a matter were to revolve between the Prophet and one's own self in any of these things, the side of the Prophet would be more preponderant than the side of one's own self.

Therefore, if any danger were to be directed toward the person of the Prophet, the believer must shield him with his own self and sacrifice his life for him. The Prophet should be more beloved to him than his own self and more honored by him than his own self. If his soul calls him to something and the Prophet calls him to the opposite, or if his soul desires something from him and the Prophet desires the opposite, it is mandatory to respond to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), obey him, and give him precedence over oneself.

Likewise, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has more right over them in matters pertaining to worldly or religious affairs; all of this is due to the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of His saying: «النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ» **The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls.**

From here, the weakness of what has been said becomes apparent: that the meaning is he has more right over them in the call (الدعوة), such that if he calls them to something and their souls call them to the opposite, they must obey him and disobey their souls. Thus, the verse would be in the sense of His saying: «وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ» **and obey the Apostle** (4:59) and His saying: «وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ» **We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave** (4:64) and similar verses. This is refuted by the absolute nature of the phrasing.

Similarly, what has been said—that the meaning is his judgment among them is more effective than the judgment of some of them over others, as in His saying: «فَسَلِّمُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِكُمْ» **greet your own souls** (24:61)—leads to the conclusion that his guardianship (ولايته) over the believers is above the guardianship of some of them over others, as indicated by His saying: «الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ بَعْضٍ» **The faithful, men and women, are allies of one another** (9:71).

However, the context does not support this interpretation.

His saying: «وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ» **and his wives are their mothers** is a legislative making (جعل تشريعي); meaning that they are in the position of their mothers regarding the obligation of honoring them and the prohibition of marrying them after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as will be explicitly stated in His saying: «وَلَا أَن تَنكِحُوا أَزْوَاجَهُ مِن بَعْدِهِ أَبَدًا» **nor that you should ever marry his wives after him.** (33:53).

Thus, the comparison (التنزيل) is only in some effects of motherhood, not in all effects, such as inheritance between them and the believers, or looking at their faces as one looks at mothers, or the prohibition of their daughters to the believers—as if they had become sisters to them—or their fathers and mothers becoming grandfathers and grandmothers, and their brothers and sisters becoming maternal uncles and aunts to the believers.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ» **Blood relatives are closer to one another in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants** (33:6) etc. **The wombs** (الأرحام) is the plural of *rahm* (رحم), which is the organ that carries the sperm until it becomes a fetus and is born. Since blood kinship (القرابة النسبية) necessarily traces back to a single womb, kinship is expressed by the term "womb," and those possessed of kinship are called "blood relatives" (أولي الأرحام).

What is meant by blood relatives being "closer to one another" is priority in inheritance (التوارث). His saying: «فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ» **in the Book of Allah** refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), the Quran, or the Surah. His saying: «مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ» **than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants** is the object of comparison. What is meant by "the faithful" here is those other than the Emigrants. The meaning is: Those possessed of kinship have more right to one another than the Emigrants and the rest of the believers who used to inherit through religious brotherhood (المؤاخاة الدينية). This priority is in the Book of Allah. It is also possible that His saying: «مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ» is an explanation (بيان) for His saying: «وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ».

The verse abrogates (ناسخة) what existed in the early days of Islam regarding inheritance through migration (الهجرة) and religious alliance (الموالاة في الدين).

His saying: «إِلَّا أَن تَفْعَلُوا إِلَىٰ أَوْلِيَائِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا» **unless you do a kindness to your friends.** (33:6). The exception is disconnected (منقطع). What is meant by "doing kindness to friends" is making a bequest (الوصية) to them from the estate, which has been legally limited to one-third of the wealth or less. His saying: «كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِي الْكِتَابِ مَسْطُورًا» **This has been written in the Book.** (33:6) means the ruling of doing kindness through a bequest is inscribed in the Preserved Tablet, the Quran, or the Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted**: «وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثَاقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا» **[Remember] when We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you, and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary, and We took from them a solemn pledge.** (33:7). The attribution of the pledge (الميثاق) to the pronoun of the prophets is evidence that what is meant is a pledge specific to them, just as mentioning them with the attribute of prophethood suggests this. Thus, the pledge taken from the prophets is a specific pledge in their capacity as prophets, and it is different from the pledge taken from all of humanity, which He refers to in His saying: «وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِن بَنِي آدَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ» **When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed!’** (7:172).

The taking of the pledge from the prophets is mentioned in another place, which is His saying: «وَإِذْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثَاقَ النَّبِيِّينَ لَمَا آتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَابٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِ وَلَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ ۚ قَالَ أَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِي ۖ قَالُوا أَقْرَرْنَا» **When Allah took a pledge from the prophets, [saying,] ‘Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom, and then an apostle comes to you confirming what is with you, you shall surely believe in him and help him.’ He said, ‘Do you agree and accept My pledge in this matter?’ They said, ‘We agree.’** (3:81).

Although the verse under discussion does not explain what the pledge taken from them was—even if it contains an allusion that it is a matter related to prophethood—it can be inferred from the verse in *Al-Imran* that the pledge was taken regarding the unity of the word in religion and the absence of disagreement therein, as in His saying: «إِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ» **Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me.** (21:92) and His saying: «شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ» **He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, [declaring,] ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’** (42:13).

He mentioned the prophets with a general term that includes all of them, then named five of them by their names through conjunction (العطف), saying: «وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ» **and from you, and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary.** The meaning of the conjunction is to distinguish them from among the rest and to specify them for mention, as if it were said: "And when We took the pledge from you five and from the rest of the prophets."

He did not specify them for mention in this manner except due to the greatness of their status and the loftiness of their position; for they are the possessors of resolve (أولوا عزم) and the masters of divine laws (شرائع) and books. He listed them according to the order of their time: Noah, then Abraham, then Moses, then Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him). However, He gave precedence to the mention of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who is the last of them in time, due to his excellence, nobility, and precedence over all.

His saying: «وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا» **and We took from them a solemn pledge** is an emphasis and intensification of the pledge, similar to His saying: «وَلَمَّا جَاءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا هُودًا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَنَجَّيْنَاهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ» **When Our edict came, We delivered Hud and those who had faith with him by a mercy from Us, and We delivered them from a harsh punishment.** (11:58).

**His saying, the Exalted**: «لِّيَسْأَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لِلْكَافِرِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا» **So that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness, and He has prepared a painful punishment for the faithless.** (33:8). The preposition *lam* in «لِّيَسْأَلَ» denotes causality (التعليل) or purpose (الغاية), and it is connected to a deleted verb indicated by His saying: «وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا» **when We took**, and His saying: «وَأَعَدَّ» **and He has prepared** is conjoined to that deleted verb. The estimation is: He did that—meaning the taking of the pledge—so that the questioning of the truthful concerning their truthfulness might be established for Him, and He prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment.

He did not say: "and so that He may prepare for the disbelievers a punishment," to intimate that their punishment is not among the final causes (العلل الغائية) for taking the pledge; rather, the deficiency is from their side and the breach is from them.

As for the questioning of the truthful concerning their truthfulness, it has been said: What is meant by "the truthful" (الصادقين) is the prophets, and questioning them about their truthfulness is questioning them on the Day of Resurrection about what their nations brought—as if taken from His saying: «يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَا أُجِبْتُمْ» **The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’** (5:109).

It has also been said: What is meant is questioning the truthful regarding the monotheism of Allah, His justice, and the divine laws about their truthfulness—meaning about what they used to say concerning it. It has been said: What is meant is questioning the truthful in their words about their truthfulness in their actions. It has been said: What is meant is questioning the truthful about what they intended by their truthfulness—was it for the sake of Allah or otherwise? Among other views, which are as you see.

Contemplation of what His saying: «لِّيَسْأَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ» **So that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness** provides, guides to something other than what they mentioned. There is a difference between saying: "I asked the wealthy man about his wealth" or "I asked the scholar about his knowledge," and saying: "I asked Zayd about his property" or "about his knowledge." What is immediately understood from the first two is that I demanded he manifest his wealth and manifest his knowledge; whereas from the latter two, it is understood that I demanded he inform me whether he has property or whether he has knowledge, or that he describe to me what property or knowledge he has.

Based on this, the meaning of questioning the truthful concerning their truthfulness is demanding that they manifest what is in their inner being of truthfulness at the level of word and deed, which is their righteous deeds (عملهم الصالح) in this world. Thus, what is meant by questioning the truthful concerning their truthfulness is directing the religious obligation (التكليف) to them according to the pledge, so that their truthfulness, which is latent in their souls, may manifest from them. This occurs in this world, not in the hereafter. Thus, the taking of the pledge occurred in another realm of existence (نشأة أخرى) before this world, as indicated by the verses of the Progeny (آيات الذر): «وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِن بَنِي آدَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ» **When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed!’** (7:172).

In short, the two verses are among those informing of the World of the Progeny (عالم الذر) — *the primordial state of existence where the souls of all human beings were brought forth and pledged their recognition of Allah's Lordship* — in which the pledge was taken. They mention that the taking of the pledge from the prophets (peace be upon them) and the arrangement of their status and actions in this world based on that, is within the context of the arrangement of the truthfulness of every truthful person based on the pledge taken from him.

Due to this generalization, He mentioned the end of the disbelievers' affair, even though they are not of the same category as the prophets and the discussion was about the pledge taken from them. It is as if it were said: "We took a solemn pledge from the prophets that their word should agree on one religion which they would convey, so that He may question the truthful and demand from them religious obligation and guidance to manifest their truthfulness in belief and action. They did so, and thus reward was decreed for them, and He prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment."

From here, the reason for the shift (الالتفات) from the first-person plural to the third-person singular in His saying: «لِّيَسْأَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ» **So that He may question the truthful** etc., becomes clear. This is because the pledge is for His worship alone without any partner, even if its taking from Him was through the mediation of the angels—which justifies the use of "We took" (أَخَذْنَا). Thus, the one who demands the truthfulness of the truthful and the one who prepares the punishment for the disbelievers is, in reality, He alone, so that He alone may be worshipped. Reflect upon this.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ» **O Prophet! Be God-conscious**—the verses were revealed concerning Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl, and Abu al-A'war al-Sulami. They came to Medina and stayed with Abdullah ibn Ubayy—after the Battle of Uhud under a guarantee of safety from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—to speak with him. They stood up, and with them stood Abdullah ibn Ubayy, Abdullah ibn Sa'id ibn Abi Sarh, and Tu'mah ibn Ubayriq. They entered upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Muhammad, abandon the mention of our goddesses al-Lat, al-Uzza, and Manat, and say: 'Indeed they have intercession for those who worship them,' and we will leave you and your Lord." This was difficult for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "Give us permission, O Messenger of Allah, to kill them." He replied: "I have given them a guarantee of safety." He then ordered that they be removed from Medina, and the verse was revealed: «وَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ» **and do not obey the faithless**—referring to the people of Mecca: Abu Sufyan, Abu al-A'war, and Ikrimah—«وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ» **and the hypocrites**—referring to Ibn Ubayy, Ibn Sa'id, and Tu'mah.

**I say**: The summary of the story is also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Jarir from Ibn Abbas. Other occasions for the revelation of the verses have been narrated, but they are extraneous and do not suit the context of the verses, so we have omitted them.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ» **nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons**: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi Umayr from Jamil from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The reason for that was that when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, he went out to the market of Ukaz for trade and saw Zayd being sold. He saw him as a clever and robust boy and bought him. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commissioned as a prophet, he called him to Islam, and he accepted. He used to be called Zayd, the ward of Muhammad.

When the news of his son Zayd reached Harithah ibn Shurahil al-Kalbi, he came to Mecca—and he was an honorable man. He came to Abu Talib and said: 'O Abu Talib, my son was taken captive, and I have heard that he is with your nephew. Ask him either to sell him, or to accept a ransom for him, or to manumit him.'

Abu Talib spoke to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Messenger of Allah said: 'He is free; let him go wherever he wishes.' Harithah stood up and took Zayd by the hand and said to him: 'O my son, join your honor and your lineage.' Zayd replied: 'I shall not leave the Messenger of Allah.' His father said to him: 'Will you leave your honor and your lineage and be a slave to Quraysh?' Zayd said: 'I shall not leave the Messenger of Allah as long as I live.' His father became angry and said: 'O people of Quraysh, bear witness that I have disowned him and he is not my son.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) then said: 'Bear witness that Zayd is my son; I shall inherit from him and he shall inherit from me.' Thus, Zayd used to be called the son of Muhammad, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) loved him and named him Zayd al-Hubb (the Beloved).

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) migrated to Medina, he married him to Zaynab bint Jahsh. One day, Zayd was delayed, so the Messenger of Allah came to his house to ask about him. He found Zaynab sitting in the middle of her room preparing her perfume with a stone pestle. The Messenger of Allah pushed the door and looked at her—and she was beautiful and fair—and said: 'Glory be to Allah, the Lord of Light, and blessed be Allah, the best of creators.' Then the Messenger of Allah returned to his house, and Zaynab occupied a remarkable place in his heart.

Zayd came to his house, and Zaynab informed him of what the Messenger of Allah had said. Zayd said to her: 'Would you like me to divorce you so that the Messenger of Allah may marry you?' She replied: 'I fear that you will divorce me and the Messenger of Allah will not marry me.' Zayd came to the Messenger of Allah and said: 'May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah, Zaynab informed me of such and such. Would you like me to divorce her so that you may marry her?' The Messenger of Allah said to him: 'No, go and be God-conscious and keep your wife.' Then Allah recounted this, saying: «أَمْسِكْ عَلَيْكَ زَوْجَكَ وَاتَّقِ اللَّهَ وَتُخْفِي فِي نَفْسِكَ مَا اللَّهُ مُبْدِيهِ وَتَخْشَى النَّاسَ وَاللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَاهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ زَيْدٌ مِّنْهَا وَطَرًا زَوَّجْنَاكَهَا» **Keep your wife for yourself and be God-conscious of Allah,’ and you were hiding in your heart what Allah was to expose, and you feared the people, though Allah is worthier that you should fear Him. So when Zayd had given up all desire for her, We gave her to you in marriage**—until His saying—«وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا» **and Allah’s command is bound to be fulfilled.** (33:37). Thus, Allah married him to her from above His Throne.

The hypocrites said: 'He forbids us from the wives of our sons, yet he marries the wife of his son Zayd.' So Allah revealed regarding this: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ» **nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons**—until His saying—«يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ» **He guides to the [right] way.**"

**I say**: Something similar to this, with some differences, is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, Abu Dawud, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he used to say: **أنا أولى بكل مؤمن من نفسه ـ فأيما رجل مات وترك دينا فالي ، ومن ترك مالا فهو لورثته** — "I am closer to every believer than his own soul. Whosoever dies and leaves a debt, it is upon me; and whosoever leaves wealth, it is for his heirs."

**I say**: There are other narrations with this meaning from both Shi'i and Sunni paths.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, and al-Nasa'i narrated from Buraydah, who said: "I went on a campaign with Ali to Yemen and saw some coldness from him. When I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), I mentioned Ali and spoke disparagingly of him. I saw the face of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) change, and he said: 'O Buraydah, am I not closer to the believers than their own souls?' I said: 'Yes, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: **من كنت مولاه فعلي مولاه** — 'Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master.'"

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Abdullah ibn Ja'far ibn Abi Talib in a long tradition, he said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'I am closer to the believers than their own souls. Whomsoever I am closer to than his own soul, then you are closer to him than his own soul'—and Ali was before him in the house."

**I say**: It is narrated in *al-Kafi* with its chain from Ja'far from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The traditions with this meaning from the paths of both groups are beyond counting.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Hannan, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'What is there for the wards (الموالي)?' He replied: 'They have nothing of the inheritance except what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: «إِلَّا أَن تَفْعَلُوا إِلَىٰ أَوْلِيَائِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا» **unless you do a kindness to your friends.**'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "It was said: 'O Messenger of Allah, when was your pledge taken?' He replied: **وآدم بين الروح والجسد** — 'While Adam was between the spirit and the body.'"

**I say**: This is narrated with its exact wording through various paths from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Its meaning is that the pledge was taken in a realm of existence other than this one and prior to it.
